1.synthesis:
After the full weight of covenant blessings and curses has been set before Israel, Moses turns — before they have even entered the land — to the promise of restoration from exile. When all the blessings and curses have come upon them in the nations where the LORD has scattered them, and when they "call them to mind" and return to the LORD with all their heart and soul, then the LORD will restore their fortunes and gather them from all the peoples and nations where he has scattered them (vv. 1-3). Even if they are at the farthest edge of the heavens, God will gather them from there (v. 4). He will bring them into the land, multiply them, circumcise their hearts, and put the curse on their enemies (vv. 5-7).

The critical promise is in verse 6: "The LORD your God will circumcise your heart and the heart of your offspring, so that you will love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul, that you may live." Keil and Delitzsch identify this as "the heart of the whole promise": the problem of Israel's stubbornness is not merely behavioral but cardiac — the uncircumcised heart (Deut 10:16) must become a divinely circumcised heart. This is the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26 prefigured in Moses.
1.historical_context: Matthew Henry identifies this passage as describing what happened in the Babylonian exile and its aftermath: Jewish exiles in Babylon "called to mind" their situation (v. 1), Daniel and others prayed and confessed (Daniel 9), and God moved the heart of Cyrus to permit their return (Ezra 1:1-4). Keil and Delitzsch, however, note that the full scope of the promise — circumcised hearts, perfect obedience, prosperity beyond anything previously known (vv. 8-10) — was not realized in the post-exilic return and must be eschatological in its ultimate fulfillment. The passage thus functions on two levels simultaneously: a pattern fulfilled in the Babylonian restoration and a promise awaiting fuller realization.
1.literary_structure: Verses 1-3 describe the human side of restoration: Israel in exile, calling to mind the blessings and curses, returning to the LORD. Verses 4-9 describe the divine side: God gathering, bringing, multiplying, circumcising hearts, and prospering. Verse 10 states the condition: "if you obey the voice of the LORD your God, to keep his commandments and his statutes that are written in this Book of the Law." But the condition of verse 10 is fulfilled by the divine circumcision of verse 6 — God provides what he requires. The passage is thus graciously circular: God enables the obedience he commands.
1.theological_application: Gill finds in verse 6 the clearest anticipation of regeneration in the Pentateuch: the circumcision of the heart is not human work but divine surgery, producing the love of God that the law demands but cannot produce. Matthew Henry applies this to every age: "The grace of God enables us to repent; it is not the work of the flesh but of the Spirit. We cannot turn without being turned; we cannot seek God unless he first seek us." Calvin notes that the promise of restored obedience (vv. 8-9) follows the promise of the circumcised heart (v. 6) — demonstrating that genuine covenant keeping is always the fruit of grace, not its precondition. The covenant is renewed not on the basis of Israel's improved performance but on the basis of God's transforming work.
1.christology: The "circumcision of the heart" promised in verse 6 is the Old Testament ground for Paul's distinction between external circumcision and the circumcision "of the heart, by the Spirit" (Rom 2:29). Colossians 2:11-12 identifies this heart-circumcision with union with Christ in his death and resurrection: "In him also you were circumcised with a circumcision made without hands, by putting off the body of the flesh, by the circumcision of Christ." The return from exile into the land (vv. 3-5) prefigures the restoration of believers to fellowship with God through Christ — the true "returning" after the exile of sin. Matthew Henry observes that the gathering "from the farthest edge of the heavens" (v. 4) anticipates Matthew 24:31, where the Son of Man sends his angels to gather his elect "from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other."

11.synthesis:
The final movement of Moses's great address is a passionate appeal to choose. The commandment he is giving them is "not too hard for you, neither is it far off" (v. 11): it is not in heaven (requiring someone to ascend and bring it down, v. 12), nor beyond the sea (requiring a distant journey, v. 13) — "But the word is very near you. It is in your mouth and in your heart, so that you can do it" (v. 14). Then the choice is set forth in the starkest terms: "I have set before you today life and good, death and evil" (v. 15). Love the LORD, walk in his ways, keep his commandments, and you will live and multiply in the land (vv. 16-18). But if your heart turns away, you will perish (v. 18). Heaven and earth are called as witnesses (v. 19): "Choose life, that you and your offspring may live."

Matthew Henry calls this "the strongest and most earnest exhortation that ever any people received" — the entire history of Moses's ministry distilled into two words: Choose life. Calvin observes the logical weight of "not too hard": there is no excuse from difficulty, no plea of impossibility. The word is near. The choice is clear. What remains is the will.
11.historical_context: The "not beyond the sea" language (v. 13) reflects the ancient cosmological sense that wisdom and divine things are distant, requiring heroic journeys to attain — as in the Gilgamesh Epic, where Utnapishtim lives "at the mouth of the rivers" far from humanity. Moses's point is that the law of God does not require such a cosmic quest: the Creator has brought his word near, writing it in human language, speaking it from a mountain that humans could climb. The invocation of heaven and earth as witnesses (v. 19) repeats the opening of Moses's Song (ch. 32:1) and signals the formal solemnity of the covenant oath.
11.literary_structure: Verses 11-14 remove the excuse of distance: the word is accessible (not in heaven, not across the sea, but in your mouth and heart). Verses 15-18 present the binary choice in three formulations: life/good vs. death/evil (v. 15), love/walk/keep vs. turn away/not hear (vv. 16-18). Verses 19-20 are the address's climax: the solemn call to witnesses, the exhortation "choose life," and the definition of choosing life as loving the LORD, obeying his voice, and holding fast to him. The passage ends with Moses's last public words before his song: "He is your life and length of days, that you may dwell in the land."
11.theological_application: Gill emphasizes that "the word is in your heart" (v. 14) describes the accessibility of the commandment — it does not require supernatural revelation at every moment but has already been heard, received, and is subject to human will. Matthew Henry distinguishes between the law's moral accessibility (it is near, clear, doable in principle) and its spiritual enablement (which requires the circumcised heart of v. 6). Calvin insists that the command to "choose" is genuine — it presupposes real human agency and responsibility, not a puppet-show divine determinism — but that the grace which enables genuine choice is prior to the choice itself. The two truths (command and grace) are held together without canceling each other.
11.christology: Paul quotes Deuteronomy 30:12-14 in Romans 10:6-8, applying the "near word" directly to the gospel: "Do not say in your heart, 'Who will ascend into heaven?' (that is, to bring Christ down) or 'Who will descend into the abyss?' (that is, to bring Christ up from the dead). But what does it say? 'The word is near you, in your mouth and in your heart' (that is, the word of faith that we proclaim)." Paul's application reveals the Deuteronomy passage's deeper referent: the word that is near is ultimately Christ himself — the Word made flesh who has already descended, died, and risen. The choice Moses presents ("choose life") is fulfilled in the New Testament call to confess Jesus as Lord and believe in his resurrection (Rom 10:9), the ultimate "choosing life."
